_doc: Composer catalog — the authoring agent reads THIS first. For each beat, try to express it as a scene_template below; only if nothing fits, author a bespoke scene (type 'raw'). Keys under scene_templates MUST equal compose.py BLOCKS (check_catalog.py enforces). REVEAL TIMING: you never hand-time per-element reveals — the composer spreads every data block's reveals (bars/items/slices/rows) across its full window (no front-loading). To land a specific element ON the words that describe it, give that element an `at` = the short spoken phrase it illustrates; the aligner syncs the reveal to narration ('show it as you say it'), and un-`at`'d elements are spread. For numbers, anchor `at` on the CONTEXT WORDS beside the number (Whisper writes numbers as digits). A `raw` scene must schedule reveals with the shared helpers (_reveal_times/_reveal_dur/_reveal_cues), never a hardcoded start+i*step (check_reveal_sync.py enforces). AMBIENT GROUND: any data-viz block (chart/pie/sankey/funnel/quadrant/cycle/spectrum/scale/spans/venn/connection_board) accepts an optional `data.ground` — a media_ground spec {kind:image, src, kenburns:[1.0,1.12]} for a slow-drifting photo, or {kind:paper} for a light crumpled-paper stock, or {kind:transparent} over a root video. It renders BEHIND the data as a living backdrop so a long hold isn't a dead flat field; a legibility veil in the theme surface colour keeps lines + numbers readable (soften with ground.dim 0..1, default 0.6). Reach for it on a data block that must hold 8s+; skip it when the data itself carries the motion. NUMBER PROVENANCE (hard gate): a data-viz block's displayed numbers must trace to the SOURCE — they are legitimate only if (a) spoken in the narration (the VO says them), (b) carry a `value_source` citation (element-level or scene-level `data.value_source`), or (c) come from a bound dataset. NEVER invent numbers to fill a block that demands data the script didn't give (a sankey needs weights, a pie needs shares) — the finish gate REJECTS a block whose numbers trace to nothing. If you have categories but no real proportions, use a NON-QUANTITATIVE block (bullet_list / diagram / connection_board), not a fake weighted sankey/pie/chart. DATASET BINDING (real numbers, never typed): instead of hand-typing a data block's numbers, BIND it to a project dataset — `data.dataset` (an id in datasets/index.json) + `data.query` (filter/sort/aggregate/derive incl. time-series pct_change/cumsum/rolling) + `data.encode` (which columns map to the block's fields: chart {x,y}, pie/stat {label,value}, sankey {label,value,source_label}). The pipeline resolves it to real cells + stamps `value_source`, so the block CAN'T fabricate — this is how you satisfy the number-provenance gate for a block the script doesn't quantify.

stat.purpose: 1-3 count-up numerals with labels + one yellow underline sweep — a data callout.
stat.when_to_use: The beat's argument IS a number or small set of numbers: a total, a rate, a multiple, a share.
stat.not_for: A drawn chart/trend line (no template yet — go raw, GSAP+SVG per data-in-motion.md). More than 3 numbers.

data_schema.kicker: string — small eyebrow label (design intent, not narration)
data_schema.register: enum(paper|footage) — ink-on-mist, or white text for a media ground
data_schema.ground: object — a media_ground spec: {kind: paper|image|transparent, parchment?, src?, kb?}
data_schema.items: array of {from,to | value, prefix?, suffix?, label, cue?, at? (spoken phrase to sync this reveal to), underline?, delta?} where delta={dir:up|down|flat, value:string} renders a colored metric-change chip (▲/▼/→) below the label
data_schema.props: array of prop_cutout specs (optional evidence overlays)
data_schema.reveal: enum(rise|char|word|flip|typewriter|scramble|decode|gradient|glitch)? — text-entrance style applied to the LABELS (numerals keep their count-up); see the 'reveals' catalog. Seek-safe.
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): stack-left|centered-row|hero-single|lead-rail|stacked-list. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills items from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {value:'col', label:'col', prefix?, suffix?} — column → each stat's number/label

statement.purpose: A short statement across 1-3 lines with ONE yellow-block sweep on the operative word, optional caption bar.
statement.when_to_use: A rhetorical/turn beat carried by words, not a number (a claim, a reveal, a 'but…').
statement.not_for: A number beat (use stat). A full paragraph (visible text stays short — the karaoke caption carries the sentence).

data_schema.ground: object — media_ground spec (default {kind: transparent} to reveal a root video)
data_schema.lines: array of strings — the statement, one entry per visual line
data_schema.operative: string — the substring in lines to highlight-sweep (must appear verbatim in a line)
data_schema.cue: number — seconds into scene for the sweep
data_schema.reveal: enum(rise|char|word|flip|typewriter|scramble|decode|gradient|glitch) — how the text enters (default rise); see the 'reveals' catalog below. All seek-safe; operative sweep still fires after the text lands.
data_schema.capCue: number? — seconds offset for the optional captionBar chip's fade-in (default 0; only relevant when captionBar is set).
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): editorial-left|centered|framed-card|banner-top|rail-accent. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.

geo.purpose: A d3 choropleth (US states or world countries) with one+ regions highlighted, pin + leader + Vox label.
geo.when_to_use: A beat about a PLACE or MOVEMENT on a map: highlight where something is (states/countries), OR draw ROUTE ARCS for migration / trade / a campaign / spread across a map.
geo.not_for: Non-geographic data (use stat/raw). A region with no TopoJSON shipped in vendor/.

data_schema.kind: enum(us|world) — us takes FIPS ids + geoAlbersUsa; world takes ISO-numeric ids + geoNaturalEarth1
data_schema.highlight: array of id strings — regions to fill (multi-region ok)
data_schema.primary: string? — which highlighted region gets the pin+leader (default highlight[0])
data_schema.title: string — the big label
data_schema.routes: array of {from:[lon,lat], to:[lon,lat], label?, hl?, bulge?}? — animated ROUTE ARCS on the map (migration, trade, campaigns, spread): a curved arrow draws from→to with an arrowhead + endpoint dots. from/to are [longitude, latitude]. A geo needs `highlight` OR `routes`.

geo.requires: vendor/ at project root: d3.min.js + topojson-client.min.js + us-states.js (kind us) / world.js (kind world). compose_frame injects the <script>s automatically.

timeline.purpose: A Vox-style horizontal timeline: a yellow spine draws in, the camera pans node-to-node, each event pops a node + elbow connector to a circular image cutout (stroked ring draw) with a big year + label. Dark ground.
timeline.when_to_use: A beat that walks a SEQUENCE OF DATED EVENTS in time — a history, a chronology, 'then... then... then', milestones.
timeline.not_for: A single moment, a non-temporal list, or numeric callouts (use stat/statement). Data with no year/order.

data_schema.events: array of {year, label?, image?, side?(up|down)} — the dated milestones, in order (side auto-alternates up/down if omitted)
data_schema.title: string? — a persistent frame title (dark register)
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight yellow
data_schema.focus: number? — screen x the active node pans to (default 640)
data_schema.gap: number? — world spacing between events in px (default 760)
data_schema.axis: enum(horizontal|vertical)? — spine orientation (default horizontal). PREFER `variant` (horizontal|vertical|vertical-up) as the interface; axis/dir are the low-level override that wins if set explicitly.
data_schema.dir: enum(down|up)? — vertical-axis travel direction (default down); only meaningful when axis=vertical.
data_schema.solo: bool? — reveal ONE event's graph at a time (the opposite side stays clear) instead of building all nodes at once — a focused, one-beat-per-event walk.
data_schema.solo_side: enum(up|down)? — which side the solo graph sits on (solo mode only).
data_schema.spine: number? — the spine's screen-x in px (default 960 = screen centre).
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): horizontal|vertical|vertical-up. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.

timeline.note: Dark ground (not the mist/paper register). event.image paths must be real assets in the project; omit for a placeholder disc.

raw.purpose: Bespoke passthrough — the 'go bespoke' escape hatch when no template fits.
raw.when_to_use: A one-off, art-directed beat with no template match (cold open, a drawn chart, a custom layout).
raw.not_for: Anything a template above already covers — prefer the template for speed + consistency.

data_schema.html: string | array of strings — the scene's clip HTML fragment(s); ids MUST be prefixed with the scene id; every timed element class='clip' with data-start/duration/track-index.
data_schema.tl: array of strings — GSAP lines (frame-absolute times) merged into the frame's one paused timeline.

bullet_list.purpose: A titled list of 3-6 key points, each led by the theme's bullet marker, staggered in — the classic explainer 'takeaways / steps / features' beat.
bullet_list.when_to_use: The beat enumerates a small set of parallel points (takeaways, steps, features, reasons) read as a list, not a paragraph or a single statement.
bullet_list.not_for: A single claim (use statement). A number/data callout (use stat/chart). More than ~6 items (split the beat).

data_schema.kicker: string - small eyebrow label (design intent, not narration)
data_schema.title: string? - the list's heading
data_schema.titleHi: string? - a substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.items: array of (string | {text, sub?}) - the points; sub is an optional secondary line
data_schema.numbered: bool? — render an ordinal (1,2,3…) before each item (auto-on for the numbered-rail variant).
data_schema.register: enum(paper|footage)? - ink-on-paper (default) or light text over a media ground
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): stack|centered|two-col|numbered-rail|card-row. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills items from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.

bullet_list.theme_notes: marker glyph = --bullet-marker (square/em-dash/chevron/dot per theme); text uses the display type role + type-scale.

pull_quote.purpose: An attributed pull-quote — an oversized decorative quote-mark glyph, the quote in the theme's display type, and a mono attribution line.
pull_quote.when_to_use: The beat IS a quotation or a single memorable line attributed to a source (an expert, a study, a person). Distinct from statement (an un-attributed manifesto/claim).
pull_quote.not_for: An un-attributed thesis (use statement). A data callout (stat/chart). A list (bullet_list).

data_schema.hi: string? - a substring of quote to highlight with the accent
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): centered|editorial|side-mark|framed. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.

pull_quote.theme_notes: quote-mark + highlight = --accent; quote uses the display type role + type-scale + display-style; cite = mono.

comparison_table.purpose: A tabular comparison MATRIX — column headers x row labels x cells, with state chips (yes/partial/no) + an optional highlighted 'ours' column.
comparison_table.when_to_use: a structured many-way comparison: options x criteria, a spec/feature table, a yes/partial/no matrix. Distinct from `comparison` (a 2-panel split).
comparison_table.not_for: a two-way this-vs-that (use comparison / split-screen); a uniform item grid (swiss-grid).

data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.columns: array of {label, highlight?} - the compared options (one may be highlighted)
data_schema.rows: array of {label, cells:[state|text]} - each criterion row; a cell of 'yes'|'no'|'partial' renders a chip, else text
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): matrix|centered|panel|compact. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.

comparison_table.theme_notes: header rule + highlight column = --accent/--surface-3; chips = --accent (yes) / --rule (no) / --accent-soft (partial); row labels = display type.

ledger.purpose: A dense hairline-separated row-list — ordinal + title + description + meta per row. A TOC / index / agenda / catalogue.
ledger.when_to_use: a table of contents, an index, an agenda, a chaptered/catalogued list. Distinct from bullet_list (flat) and swiss-grid (2D cards).
ledger.not_for: a short bulleted list (bullet_list); 2D item cards (swiss-grid).

data_schema.rows: array of {title, desc?, meta?, num?} - num defaults to a 01/02 ordinal
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): rows|two-col|centered|boxed. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills rows from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {text:'col', value?:'col'} — column → each row's title (+ optional meta value)

ledger.theme_notes: ordinal = --accent + --hero-num style; title = display type; meta = mono; hairline rows = --rule under a heavier --accent top rule.

newshead.purpose: A newspaper headline card (Vox 'newspaper animation'): dot-grid paper, red date tag, bold serif headline whose words cascade in with a yellow highlighter sweep, a serif subhead (optional highlight), a blackletter source masthead + drawn rule, an optional framed grayscale photo sliding in, and an optional red hand-drawn arrow annotation.
newshead.when_to_use: A beat that shows a REAL (or styled) NEWS HEADLINE / press clipping — 'the story broke', citing an article/report, a scandal, a quote-in-the-news.
newshead.not_for: A plain on-screen statement (use statement), a stat/number (stat), or a photo with no headline framing.

data_schema.headline: array of strings (one per visual line) OR a string — words cascade in
data_schema.reveal: enum(rise|char|word|flip|typewriter|scramble|decode|gradient|glitch)? — override the native word-cascade with a reveal style on the HEADLINE (the highlight phrase becomes the operative sweep); see the 'reveals' catalog. Seek-safe.
data_schema.highlight: string? — a phrase within the headline to sweep-highlight yellow
data_schema.subhead: string? — the standfirst/dek paragraph below the headline
data_schema.subhighlight: string? — a phrase within the subhead to highlight yellow (static)
data_schema.source: string? — masthead, e.g. 'The New York Times' (blackletter) + a drawn rule
data_schema.image: string? — a real asset path; framed grayscale photo on the right
data_schema.caption: string? — italic caption under the photo
data_schema.arrow: bool? — draw a red hand-drawn annotation arrow toward the photo
data_schema.tilt: number? — small rotation of the whole card in degrees (e.g. -2), to make it less flat
data_schema.image_tilt: number? — extra rotation of the photo (relative to the card), e.g. 5 for a pinned-askew look
data_schema.cutout: bool? — treat image as a transparent-PNG SUBJECT (grayscale + red outline) that bleeds off the card's right edge, instead of a framed photo. Needs a cutout PNG: `nolan cutout -m birefnet -o ` (trim the alpha bbox / drop reflections).

newshead.note: Dark ground; the card is the paper. image must be a real asset in the project. headline is required.

collage.purpose: A kinetic COLLAGE: cut-out subjects (people/objects, transparent PNGs) scale/slide in staggered and assemble into a layered tableau on a backdrop, then hold. Borderless cutouts composited into one scene — NOT framed photos on a table (that's a montage).
collage.when_to_use: Assembling a CAST or an evidence set of people/objects into one composed scene — 'here are the players', a lineup, a still-life of the things the narration names.
collage.not_for: A single subject (use newshead cutout / still), framed photos in sequence (montage), or text/data beats.

data_schema.subjects: array of {src (transparent PNG cutout), x?,y? (0..1 rest center), scale?, rotation?, from?:left|right|top|bottom|center, at? (enter cue s), shadow?} — explicit position, or omit x/y to use `layout`
data_schema.backdrop: string? — CSS color (default #ffffff), a texture image path, or 'transparent'
data_schema.vignette: number? — edge darkening 0..1 (default 0; use on dark backdrops)
data_schema.layout: string? — preset arrangement when subjects omit x/y: row(default)|heroes|heroes-tight|cluster|scatter (heroes-tight = closer/bigger flanking subjects + an overlapping cast row, for a tight ensemble)
data_schema.camera: string? — 'push' for a slow Ken-Burns over the whole collage; else static

collage.note: Subjects must be real transparent-PNG cutouts in the project: `nolan cutout -m birefnet -o ` then trim the alpha bbox. Quality lives on cutout quality.

diagram.purpose: A node-link DIAGRAM laid out by d3 (tree / flow / radial) and revealed by GSAP: nodes pop in parent→child order, each connector draws in. Ink cards on mist, or a dark register.
diagram.when_to_use: A beat that shows STRUCTURE or a PROCESS: how a system is organised, a pipeline/flow (A→B→C), a hierarchy/taxonomy, a decision tree, 'how X works', what-splits-into-what.
diagram.not_for: A drawn data chart / trend line (no template yet — go raw). A single labelled image. A dated chronology (use timeline). Non-hierarchical prose.

data_schema.root: object {label, sub?, hl?, shape?:pill, children?:[ …same shape… ]} — the graph as a nested hierarchy; the root node is auto-styled as a dark chip
data_schema.layout: enum(tree|flow|radial) — tree = vertical (default), flow = horizontal process, radial = hub-and-spoke
data_schema.dir: string? — orientation within the layout: tree → down(default)|up; flow → right(default)|left
data_schema.camera: string? — 'tour' = a zoom-into-a-canvas-blanket walk: the camera frames a node, its children branch out, then it dives into a child and repeats (DFS pre-order) — a big tree reveals as an endless focus→branch→dive. Default: fit the whole graph statically.
data_schema.space: string? — '3d' lays the network on a TILTED, receding grid plane (cards stand up as billboards, glowing connectors on the plane) — the cinematic perspective look. Composes with camera:'tour' -> the 3D camera FLIES node-to-node (focus -> branch out -> dive), else a steady fly-over glide. Default: flat 2D (where layout/dir also apply).
data_schema.title: string? — big frame title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight yellow
data_schema.shape: enum(pill)? — rounded-pill nodes instead of cards (per-node override via root.*.shape)

diagram.requires: vendor/d3.min.js at project root (compose_frame injects the <script> automatically). d3 lays out ONCE at load; no d3.transition/forceSimulation (Math.random) — GSAP owns all motion.
diagram.note: Deterministic layouts only. Node labels are short (a phrase), never a narration sentence. Keep the tree shallow enough to read (≈≤10 nodes).

comparison.purpose: A split A-vs-B / before-after VISUAL CONTRAST: two panels (side-by-side or stacked), a divider that draws + an optional center VS badge, each panel sliding in. Each side is a VISUAL — an image (Ken-Burns + effects) or a framework video; a title/kicker and a stat number ride only as OVERLAYS.
comparison.when_to_use: A beat that CONTRASTS two things VISUALLY: this photo vs that photo, before vs after footage, the map then vs now — where each side is an image or video.
comparison.not_for: A text-vs-text / stat-vs-stat / stat-vs-text contrast (use `juxtaposition`). A single subject (use statement/stat/media_ground). More than two things (use a diagram or gallery).

data_schema.left: object — the first side's VISUAL spec (image|video; see side spec)
data_schema.right: object — the second side's VISUAL spec
data_schema.axis: enum(vertical|horizontal) — vertical = side-by-side (default), horizontal = stacked top/bottom
data_schema.style: enum(seamless|framed) — seamless = panels tile with a thin divider (default); framed = bordered cards with a gap on a backdrop
data_schema.backdrop: color? — canvas/gap fill behind framed cards (default: the theme surface); also the body colour a video side shows against
data_schema.vs: bool | string? — center badge; true → 'VS', or a custom short string
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over a centered top title
data_schema.title: string? — a centered frame title spanning both panels
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight yellow
data_schema._side_spec: each side: {type:image|video, label?, …}. label = a corner chip (e.g. 'BEFORE'). image {src, kenburns?:[from,to]|bool (default on), scrim?, vignette?:0..1, grayscale?, tint?:color, kicker?, title?, highlight?}; video {src, media_start?, scrim?, grayscale?, kicker?, title?, highlight?} (mounted at index root). OVERLAY on either: stat {from,to | value, prefix?, suffix?, label?} → a big number burned on the media. A pure text/stat SIDE is rejected by the gate → use `juxtaposition`.
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): split|cards|over-under|cards-stacked. Vary it across beats so the video doesn't render the same shape twice; see themes/composition/layout_variants.json.

comparison.note: A `video` side renders as a TRANSPARENT HOLE (the framework can't drive <video> inside a sub-comp); after composing, run `python bridge/inject_comparison_videos.py <project>` to mount each clip as a real <video> at the index root, positioned to the panel rect (archetype B). So a video-vs-video is two stacked root videos with the frame's chrome on top. image/text/stat are fully self-contained (no inject step). All src/image paths must be real assets; on-screen text stays short (caption track carries narration).

juxtaposition.purpose: A NON-VISUAL A-vs-B: two claims or two numbers set against each other on a typographic axis — two zones slide in on the theme surface, a rule draws between them, an optional centre VS/pivot lands. Editorial display type, accent operative. This is the rhetorical/numeric contrast comparison is NOT for.
juxtaposition.when_to_use: A beat that CONTRASTS two things in WORDS or NUMBERS: what they promised vs what it cost, 12% vs 88%, the claim vs the rebuttal, then vs now as figures — where neither side is an image.
juxtaposition.not_for: A VISUAL contrast — two photos/videos (use `comparison`). A single claim (use `statement`). A single number (use `stat`). More than two things (use a diagram/ledger/bullet_list).

data_schema.axis: enum(vertical|horizontal)? — vertical = side-by-side (default), horizontal = stacked top/bottom
data_schema.vs: bool | string? — centre pivot badge; true → 'VS', or a custom short word ('BUT', '=', '→')
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over a centered top title
data_schema.title: string? — a centered frame title spanning both zones
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.backdrop: color? — ground fill (default: the theme surface)
data_schema._side_spec: each side: text {kicker?, lines:str|[lines] (or title), highlight?, reveal?:rise|char|word|flip|typewriter|scramble|decode|gradient|glitch}; stat {from,to | value, prefix?, suffix?, label?, kicker?}
data_schema.variant: optional layout arrangement (omit to auto-pick by content + avoid repeating the previous beat's): split|stacked|cards|cards-stacked. See themes/composition/layout_variants.json.

juxtaposition.note: Split-screen archetype (textual). Fully self-contained (no inject step). On-screen text stays short — the caption track carries narration; these are the operative CONTRAST words/numbers only.

gallery.purpose: A grid or masonry of framed images that reveal in a staggered cascade (center-out), then can SPOTLIGHT one image — it scales up + lifts while the rest dim + blur (background-blur emphasis).
gallery.when_to_use: A beat that shows a SET of images at once — a wall of examples / faces / screenshots / evidence, a photo grid, 'here are the images' — then optionally zooming attention onto one of them.
gallery.not_for: A single image (use media_ground / newshead / a still). Borderless cut-out subjects assembling into one scene (use collage). Images shown strictly one-at-a-time in sequence (a montage — not yet a template).

data_schema.images: array of {src, caption?} OR bare src strings — the framed photos (real asset paths); 2-9 works best
data_schema.cols: number? — grid columns (default auto by image count)
data_schema.layout: enum(grid|masonry) — grid = uniform cells (default); masonry = varied heights, column-packed
data_schema.frame: bool? — white matte card around each image (default true); false = borderless bleed
data_schema.backdrop: string? — CSS color (default '#17181A'), a texture image path, or 'transparent'
data_schema.title: string? — a persistent frame title above the grid
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight yellow
data_schema.captions: bool? — show per-image captions under each card (default false)
data_schema.highlight: number? — 0-based index of ONE image to spotlight after the reveal
data_schema.highlight_at: number? — seconds into scene for the spotlight (default just after the reveal ends)
data_schema.highlight_caption: string? — an italic caption revealed under the spotlighted image

gallery.note: images must be real assets in the project. The staggered reveal is transform/opacity only; the OPTIONAL spotlight adds a deterministic filter:blur hold on the non-hero cards (still seek-safe — no Math.random, no from:'random', no repeat:-1). Keeps content above the ~83% caption keep-out. layout:masonry column-packs varied heights and fit-scales them into the grid box.

carousel.purpose: A TEMPORAL sequence of images, auto-advancing with one in focus — the moving cousin of gallery. style:slider = full-bleed one-at-a-time (kenburns/crossfade); style:coverflow = a 3D coverflow that steps focus card to card.
carousel.when_to_use: A beat that WALKS THROUGH a set of images in sequence — a slideshow while the narration lists things, a 'flip through' of covers/screens/photos, a montage that lands on each in turn.
carousel.not_for: All images at once in a grid (use gallery). A single image (media_ground / newshead / a still). Non-image sequences.

data_schema.images: array of {src, caption?} OR bare src strings — the ordered slides (real asset paths); 3-7 works best
data_schema.style: enum(slider|coverflow) — slider = full-bleed or a card scroll (see layout); coverflow = 3D coverflow, centre card forward
data_schema.layout: enum(full|cards) — SLIDER only: full (default) = one at a time (full-bleed, OR a centered SIZED card if you set card_w/card_h); cards = a horizontal card scroll (row of framed cards, track slides so the active card centres, neighbours peek)
data_schema.transition: enum(kenburns|crossfade) — slider layout:full only; kenburns (default) = crossfade + a slow per-slide zoom drift
data_schema.hold: number? — seconds each slide holds (default: scene dur spread evenly across the images)
data_schema.card_w: number? — card width in px. On slider layout:full it shrinks the slide from full-bleed to a centered card; slider layout:cards default 820; coverflow default 700
data_schema.card_h: number? — card height in px (pairs with card_w). slider layout:cards default 500; coverflow default 480
data_schema.gap: number? — px gap between cards (slider layout:cards, default 60)
data_schema.spacing: number? — coverflow: horizontal px step between cards (default 330)
data_schema.depth: number? — coverflow: translateZ px the side cards recede per step (default 175)
data_schema.fade: number? — coverflow: opacity lost per step of distance from focus — a smooth edge fade (default 0.32)
data_schema.y: number? — vertical centre as a fraction 0..1 (coverflow + slider layout:cards, default 0.5)
data_schema.backdrop: string? — CSS color (default '#101014'; shows behind coverflow cards / letterbox), image path, or 'transparent'
data_schema.title: string? — a persistent frame title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight yellow
data_schema.captions: bool? — show each slide's caption (bottom, tied to its focus window)

carousel.note: images must be real assets. Opacity + transforms only (coverflow uses rotateY/translateZ/scale in a perspective+preserve-3d stage; depth occlusion is by translateZ, not z-index). Seek-safe: NO Math.random, NO repeat:-1 (the gsapify infinite-loop was dropped). Deferred: transition:push, style:strip (marquee) / deck, focus/dwell. Captions sit above the ~81% caption keep-out.

linedraw.purpose: A self-drawing LINE-ART SVG: every stroke draws itself (stroke-dashoffset length->0) in order at a constant pen speed, so a line drawing renders as if hand-drawn. Logo reveals, blueprints, maps/routes, signatures, hand-drawn explainer icons, schematics.
linedraw.when_to_use: A beat carried by a DRAWING made of lines/strokes — a logo/icon that draws on, a route/map traced, a signature, a schematic/blueprint, a hand-illustrated concept.
linedraw.not_for: A filled/photographic image (no strokes to draw — centerline-trace it to SVG first, or use a still/collage). A data chart (no template yet). Text (use statement reveals).

data_schema.svg: string — a raw <svg>...</svg> whose stroke elements (path/line/polyline/polygon/circle/ellipse/rect) get drawn. OR use `paths`/`src`.
data_schema.paths: array of path `d` strings (with `viewBox`, default '0 0 100 100') — shorthand when you only have path data
data_schema.src: string? — path to an .svg file to embed (read at build)
data_schema.stroke: string? — stroke color in draw mode (default ink on paper / white on footage)
data_schema.strokeWidth: number? — stroke width in user units (default 3)
data_schema.order: enum(dom|length-asc|length-desc)? — draw order (default dom = document order)
data_schema.keepStyle: bool? — respect the SVG's own stroke/fill instead of forcing a uniform stroke
data_schema.ink: bool? — after each stroke draws, fade its fill in (needs keepStyle fills) — for filled illustrations
data_schema.lead: number? — delay before drawing starts (s, default 0.4)
data_schema.gap: number? — pause between consecutive strokes (s, default 0.04)

linedraw.note: No external lib — a setup IIFE measures each stroke's getTotalLength() ONCE at load (deterministic) and adds the draw tweens; strokeDashoffset is the framework's blessed seek-safe draw (same as geo/diagram/timeline). SVG should be id-free (inner ids aren't scoped). RASTER line art: centerline-trace to SVG first (potrace traces outlines, not pen strokes).

document.purpose: A page / sheet (document, article, or letter) as the subject, revealed and annotated: a single full-bleed page, a cascading multi-page stack, or sheet(s) on a backdrop with a cinematic push. Annotations = highlighter sweep, underline, summary callout card, pinned label, handwritten caption.
document.when_to_use: A beat built around a REAL PAGE: quoting/annotating a filing/report/contract, 'the story is in this document', an old letter/manuscript, a screenshotted article/headline, or 'then it's hundreds of pages' (stack).
document.not_for: A styled newspaper front (use newshead). A plain on-screen quote with no page (use statement). A data chart (use stat/diagram).

data_schema.source: string (a page image) | array of strings (pages for a stack) — a real scan/screenshot/render in the project's assets
data_schema.mode: enum(page|stack|artifact) — page = one full-bleed sheet (default); stack = several pages cascade/fan ('hundreds of pages'); artifact = sheet(s) on a backdrop (e.g. velvet) with a push
data_schema.fit: enum(cover|contain) — cover fills the sheet (default for page), contain shows the whole page (default for artifact)
data_schema.backdrop: color? — behind the sheet (default light for page, dark for artifact)
data_schema.aged: bool? — sepia/parchment tint for old paper
data_schema.camera: enum(push|scroll|static|region) — push=Ken-Burns, scroll=vertical drift, region=ZOOM into data.focus's region (B-P3), static(default)
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight
data_schema.annotations: array of {type, ...}. Position by REGION id (B-P2: {region:"p3-fig0"} — a layout-map region/word/figure id from the bound `document`; the only way to target a FIGURE), OR by text — {find:"phrase on the page"} — resolved to a rect by bridge/resolve_doc_annotations.py (PREFERRED; OCR for images, PyMuPDF text-layer for PDFs), OR by explicit fraction (the escape hatch). highlight {find|rect:[fx,fy,fw,fh], color?, cue?}; underline {find|rect:[fx,fy,fw], color?, cue?}; label {find|at:[fx,fy], text, color?, cue?} (pinned, rides the camera); callout {text:str|[lines], title?, side?:right|left, y?:0..1, cue?} (screen-fixed card); caption {text, underline?:substr, cue?} (bottom italic, red underline draw) READ-ALONG: add `read:true` (+ optional `read_dur`) to a highlight so it sweeps across the region at reading pace. VO-SYNC: `sync:"spoken phrase"` (auto-filled from a region's text) pins the annotation's cue to when that text is READ. EVIDENCE (B-P3 W2): redaction {region|rect} — a black bar that LIFTS to reveal ('the part they buried'); stamp {region|at, text, color?, angle?} — a rubber-stamp (APPROVED/CLASSIFIED) slams on; strike {region|rect, text?} — TRACK-CHANGES strikethrough + a green insertion; term {rect, gloss?, color?} — EQUATION term-by-term (highlight a term + its gloss, in sequence via cues). POLISH (B-P3 W3): margin {region|rect, text, color?} — a handwritten marginalia note + a dashed LEADER LINE to the region; pullquote {region|rect, text?, side?:center|left|right} — a sentence LIFTS off the page into a big pull-quote as the page dims (text from the region or explicit).
data_schema.page_size: [w,h]? — set by resolve_doc_annotations.py; sizes the sheet to the page aspect so annotation fractions map 1:1 (no crop). Omit for full-bleed cover.
data_schema.document: string? — an INGESTED document id (nolan/document, B-P1): binds its rendered page as `source` + page_size + provenance, and resolves `region` annotations from the layout map. Pair with `page`.
data_schema.focus: string? — a region id (from the bound document's layout map) the camera FRAMES or the lift crops. Pair with camera:'region' or focus_mode:'lift'.
data_schema.focus_mode: enum(lift)? — 'lift' clones the focus region, blows it up centre-screen and BLUR+DIMs the full page behind it (the crop→zoom→recede move). Omit for a normal in-page camera.

document.note: PREFER authoring highlight/underline/label by {find:"phrase"} and running `resolve_doc_annotations.py` (locates the phrase on the page and fills the rect + stamps page_size) — no eyeballing coordinates. Explicit fractions stay supported for regions with no matchable text (a chart area, a photo). callouts + caption are screen-fixed. source is a real screenshot/scan/render (or a .pdf, which the resolver rasterises); on-screen text stays short.

annotate.purpose: An ANNOTATED FIGURE: a photo/still fills the frame (optional Ken-Burns) and leader-line CALLOUTS label points on it — a marker drops on a region, a thin line draws out to a labelled pill, staggered. The explainer 'here's what's in this image' device.
annotate.when_to_use: A beat that POINTS OUT parts of a REAL image: labelling a photo/scene, the parts of an object or map region, an anatomy, 'notice X here and Y there' over a picture.
annotate.not_for: An abstract node/flow with no image (use `diagram`). A full-bleed image with no labels (put a `statement` over a `media_ground`). A data chart (use `chart`). A two-image contrast (use `comparison`).

data_schema.src: string — the image asset to annotate (a real path in the project)
data_schema.callouts: array of {x, y (0..1 the point ON the image), text, lx?, ly? (0..1 label position; else auto to the nearer side)} — the labelled markers, revealed in order. Keep points above the 83% caption keep-out.
data_schema.fit: enum(cover|contain)? — cover (default, fills) or contain (whole image, letterboxed)
data_schema.kb: [from,to] | bool? — Ken-Burns scale on the image (default a gentle push)
data_schema.scrim: bool? — a soft vignette for label legibility (default true)
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a top title over the image
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent

annotate.note: Callout markers/lines/labels use the theme --accent; labels are theme --surface pills. Leader lines draw via strokeDashoffset (seek-safe). src must be a real asset; on-screen label text stays short.

quadrant.purpose: A 2x2 MATRIX: two labelled axes cross the canvas into four quadrants; items plot at (x,y) and/or each quadrant carries a corner label. The axes draw out from the centre, then items pop staggered. The analytical positioning device (BCG, effort/impact, risk/reward).
quadrant.when_to_use: A beat that POSITIONS things on TWO dimensions at once: effort vs impact, cost vs quality, a 2x2 matrix, mapping trade-offs / a landscape.
quadrant.not_for: A single ranking or list (use `ledger`/`bullet_list`). A time sequence (use `timeline`). A tabular matrix of discrete options x criteria (use `comparison_table`). A one-axis magnitude (use `stat`).

data_schema.x: object — the horizontal axis: {label, lo?, hi?} (axis title + the two end labels)
data_schema.y: object — the vertical axis: {label, lo?, hi?}
data_schema.items: array of {x, y (0..1; 0 = left/bottom), label, hl?} — plotted points, revealed in order
data_schema.quadrants: object? — corner labels {tl?, tr?, bl?, br?} naming each quadrant
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a centered top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent

quadrant.note: Axes + dots use the theme --accent/--rule; item labels are theme --text with a --surface halo. Keep on-screen labels short; item (x,y) are 0..1 fractions of the plot box.

venn.purpose: A VENN / set-overlap: 2 or 3 labelled circles overlap and the intersection is labelled. Circles scale in, then labels + the centre overlap pill fade. The 'where these meet' explainer device.
venn.when_to_use: A beat about the INTERSECTION or overlap of sets: where two/three things meet, the sweet spot, ikigai, 'you need all three', shared ground between groups.
venn.not_for: A two-item contrast with no overlap (use `comparison`/`juxtaposition`). More than 3 sets, or a hierarchy (use `diagram`). A ranking or list (use `bullet_list`/`ledger`).

data_schema.sets: array of 2 or 3 {label, sub?} — the circles (sub = a small overline). 2 → side-by-side, 3 → triangle.
data_schema.overlap: string? — the centre intersection label (a pill in the shared region)
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a centered top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent

venn.note: Circles are theme --accent (tinted via color-mix so overlaps darken); the overlap pill is a theme --surface card. Keep labels short. 2 or 3 sets only — more overlaps get illegible.

sankey.purpose: A SANKEY flow: one source splits into N targets with PROPORTIONAL-width ribbons — a total and its parts shown by area. The source bar draws, ribbons fade in weighted by value, target nodes + labels land.
sankey.when_to_use: A beat that BREAKS A TOTAL into weighted parts as a FLOW: a budget → categories, energy in → uses, revenue → costs, 'where the money/time/energy goes', a whole apportioned by size.
sankey.not_for: A simple ranked list of values (use `chart` bars or `ledger`). A 2-way contrast (use `comparison`/`juxtaposition`). A hierarchy/network (use `diagram`). Multi-stage flows (this is a single split).

data_schema.source: object — the total: {label, value?} (the left bar; value is shown if given)
data_schema.targets: array of {label, value (the weight — ribbon width ∝ value), hl?} — the parts, top→bottom
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top-left title
data_schema.title: string? — a top-left title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills targets from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {label:'col', value:'col', source_label?} — column → each target's label/value; source is their sum

sankey.note: Ribbon widths are proportional to target.value; the source bar spans the full plot height. Ribbons + nodes are theme --accent (color-mix tint); a hl target uses --text. Keep 2-6 targets — more get thin.

scale.purpose: A MAGNITUDE / SCALE comparison: 2+ things drawn as circles sized by AREA (radius ∝ sqrt(value)) so relative size reads at a glance. Circles grow from a shared baseline, labels + values land, an optional ratio callout pops. The 'how big is big' device.
scale.when_to_use: A beat about RELATIVE SIZE / magnitude: this is N times bigger/smaller, orders of magnitude, 'to scale', a tiny thing next to a huge thing, comparing quantities where the SIZE is the point.
scale.not_for: Precise values to read off (use `chart`/`stat`). Parts of a whole as a flow (use `sankey`). A two-way qualitative contrast (use `comparison`/`juxtaposition`).

data_schema.items: array of {label, value (drives the circle AREA), hl?} — 2+ things, left→right
data_schema.ratio: string? — a called-out ratio placed above the tallest circle (e.g. '400x')
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a centered top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills items from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {value:'col', label:'col', suffix?} — column → each item's value/label

scale.note: Circle radius ∝ sqrt(value) so AREA is proportional (the honest size encoding); the biggest sets the scale. Circles are theme --accent, a hl item uses --text. Keep 2-5 items; huge ratios make small ones dots.

pie.purpose: A PIE / DONUT: share-of-a-whole as radial segments (who owns it, the vote split, market share). Segments sweep in staggered with a %/label legend; a donut (hole>0) can hold a centre label.
pie.when_to_use: A beat about SHARE OF A WHOLE / composition: who owns what, the vote/market/budget split, 'X% of the total is Y'. 2-6 slices.
pie.not_for: Values to compare in magnitude (use `chart` bars). A total broken into a FLOW (use `sankey`). More than ~6 slices (illegible). Precise trends over time (use `chart` line).

data_schema.segments: array of {label, value, hl?} — the slices (value = share; % auto-computed). hl = full accent.
data_schema.hole: number(0..1)? — 0 = pie (default), ~0.58 = donut (frees the centre for a label)
data_schema.center: string? — donut centre label (e.g. a total)
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills segments from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {label:'col', value:'col'} — column → each segment's label/value

pie.note: Slices are theme --accent at stepped opacity (hl = full); legend on the right. Keep 2-6 slices.

funnel.purpose: A FUNNEL: narrowing stages (TAM→SAM→SOM, leads→customers, awareness→purchase). Each stage is a trapezoid whose width ∝ value; stages drop in top→bottom with value + label.
funnel.when_to_use: A beat about a NARROWING PROCESS / conversion: a sales/marketing funnel, drop-off between stages, a pipeline that filters down, TAM→SAM→SOM.
funnel.not_for: Parts of a whole with no ordering (use `pie`). A flow that splits/branches (use `sankey`). A ranked list of independent values (use `chart` bars). A 2-way contrast (use `comparison`/`juxtaposition`).

data_schema.stages: array of {label, value, hl?} — ordered top→bottom (widest→narrowest); width ∝ value
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills stages from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {label:'col', value:'col'} — column → each stage's label/value

funnel.note: Trapezoids are theme --accent at stepped opacity (hl = full); labels ride on each stage with a --surface halo. 3-6 stages read best.

spectrum.purpose: A 1-axis SPECTRUM: items placed along a SINGLE labelled axis (a scale from lo to hi). Optional tinted zones band the axis; it draws, items pop at their x with labels alternating above/below.
spectrum.when_to_use: A beat that POSITIONS things on ONE dimension / a scale: the political spectrum, risk appetite, cheap→premium, dove→hawk, 'on a scale from X to Y here's where each falls'.
spectrum.not_for: TWO dimensions at once (use `quadrant`). Precise magnitudes (use `chart`/`scale`). A ranked list (use `bullet_list`/`ledger`). A time sequence (use `timeline`).

data_schema.axis: object — {label?, lo, hi} (the axis title + the two end labels)
data_schema.items: array of {x (0..1; 0=lo end), label, sub?, hl?} — points on the axis (labels auto-alternate above/below)
data_schema.zones: array of {x0, x1 (0..1), label?}? — tinted bands segmenting the axis (e.g. left/centre/right)
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills items from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {value:'col', label:'col'} — column → each item's value/label

spectrum.note: Axis/dots are theme --accent/--rule; a hl item uses --text. item.x is a 0..1 fraction of the axis.

cycle.purpose: A CIRCULAR process / LOOP: steps on a ring joined by curved arrows that close back on themselves. Nodes pop in sequence, arrows draw between; an optional centre label names the loop.
cycle.when_to_use: A beat about a REPEATING / self-reinforcing process: a feedback loop, a flywheel, the water/carbon cycle, a virtuous or vicious circle, a process that returns to its start.
cycle.not_for: A one-way process with an end (use `diagram` flow or `timeline`). A hierarchy (use `diagram` tree). Parts of a whole (use `pie`). A narrowing funnel (use `funnel`).

data_schema.steps: array of {label, sub?, hl?} — the loop stages, in order (arrows wrap last→first)
data_schema.center: string? — a centre label naming the loop
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent

cycle.note: Nodes are theme --surface cards (hl = accent fill); arrows are accent arcs (strokeDashoffset draw + an arrowhead marker). 3-6 steps read best on the ring.

detail_zoom.purpose: A FOCUS TOUR across ONE image: the camera pans + zooms between marked regions with a caption per stop ('look here… now here'). The art-essay close-reading move on a painting, map, or photo.
detail_zoom.when_to_use: A beat that WALKS THROUGH ONE image in detail: reading a painting region by region, touring parts of a map/diagram/photo, 'notice this… and here… and here' over a single still.
detail_zoom.not_for: Labelling all parts at once (use `annotate`). A single full-frame image (use `media_ground`). A sequence of DIFFERENT images (use `gallery`/`carousel`). A two-image contrast (use `comparison`).

data_schema.src: string — the image to tour (a real project asset; a high-res image tours best)
data_schema.stops: array of {x, y (0..1 focus point), scale?(zoom, default ~1.9), caption?, sub?} — the tour stops, in order; the camera pans/zooms to each and holds
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a top title over the image
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent

detail_zoom.note: A single transformed camera layer pans/zooms (transform-only, seek-safe); captions are theme --surface cards fading per stop. dur is split evenly across stops; src must be a real asset.

lower_third.purpose: A lower-third identifier — name + role (+ optional kicker) — the on-screen caption for talking-head / interview / podcast footage. One theme-driven block collapsing the registry's ~12 lt-* variants via a `style` param.
lower_third.when_to_use: Naming a speaker / source on screen over footage or a talking head: an interview subject, a quoted expert, a podcast guest.
lower_third.not_for: A full statement/quote (use statement), a data callout (stat), or a news headline card (newshead).

data_schema.name: string — the person/source name (the main line)
data_schema.kicker: string? — a small accent eyebrow tag above the name
data_schema.style: enum(bar|card|underline|block)? — OMIT to get a THEME-COHERENT default from the theme's type personality (elegant/editorial→underline, brutalist→block, geometric/mono→bar, rounded→card); set it only to override. bar (surface card + accent tab, slides in) | card (surface card + drawn accent underline, rises) | underline (cardless: name rises, accent rule draws L->R, text-shadowed for footage) | block (bold accent-colour block wipes in, ink name slams up)
data_schema.backdrop: string? — STANDALONE/demo only: a color or image behind it. Normally omitted (transparent over the footage/root video).

lower_third.note: Theme-driven: accent/text/surface/accent-ink from the active theme (a lower-third should match the video's brand). Sits in the lower band; normally transparent over footage. Deferred styles (side-rule/soft-pill/mask-reveal/stack-bars/news-ticker) add as new `style` branches.

chart.purpose: An animated bar or line chart — GSAP+SVG/CSS (NOT d3/Chart.js, seek-safe): bars grow from the baseline staggered / a line draws in, with value + axis labels and gridlines. Theme-driven.
chart.when_to_use: A beat whose argument is a TREND or a COMPARISON across a few categories/times — growth over years, a ranking, a before/after set. More than ~3 numbers that want a drawn shape (stat is for 1-3 callout numbers).
chart.not_for: 1-3 hero numbers (use stat). A choropleth/geographic distribution (geo). A pie/scatter/complex viz (go raw).

data_schema.type: enum(bar|line|waterfall)? — bar (default), line, or waterfall (cumulative +/- bars floating at the running total — a revenue/budget BRIDGE) — a LINE chart accepts `playhead:true` for a TIME-SERIES sweep: a vertical time-cursor sweeps left→right in sync with the draw (the beat reads as time advancing), points reveal as it reaches them.
data_schema.series: array of {label, value, total?} — bars/points; for waterfall, value is the signed DELTA and total:true makes a full bar from 0 (start/end)
data_schema.ymax: number? — y-axis max (default: auto from the data + headroom)
data_schema.highlight: number? — index of ONE bar to emphasise in the accent; the rest mute
data_schema.title: string? — a title above the plot
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight in the accent
data_schema.playhead: bool? — LINE type only: a vertical TIME-CURSOR sweeps left→right in sync with the draw so the beat reads as time advancing; points/values reveal as it reaches them (time-series).
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills series from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {x:'col', y:'col', prefix?, suffix?} — which columns become each point's label/value

chart.note: Theme-driven: bars/line + highlight use --accent; labels --text/--text-2; gridlines/baseline --rule; ground --shell. Pure GSAP+SVG (scaleY bar grow / strokeDashoffset line draw) — no chart library. For a non-bar/line viz, go raw.

code.purpose: A syntax-highlighted code window that types in or spotlights a line — tokenised in Python (no browser highlighter), reveal is seek-safe. Collapses the registry's code effects + 24 code-snippet theme profiles into ONE block + a `theme` param.
code.when_to_use: Showing SOURCE CODE / a terminal / a snippet in a tech or dev explainer: a function, a config, a diff, a command.
code.not_for: A drawn chart (chart), a plain statement (statement). Non-code text.

data_schema.code: string — the code (multi-line; \n newlines, tabs OK). Tokenised into syntax colours at build time.
data_schema.theme: enum(monokai|vs-dark|vs-light|github-dark|dracula) — the SYNTAX palette (its own identity, NOT the NOLAN video theme). Default monokai.
data_schema.mode: enum(typing|highlight) — typing (default) = lines reveal staggered; highlight = spotlight one line + dim the rest
data_schema.highlight_at: number? — seconds into scene for the spotlight (default mid)
data_schema.title: string? — a title above the window (NOLAN-themed)
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight in the accent

code.note: HYBRID theming: the editor window's syntax colours are the fixed code `theme` (inline hex); the scene backdrop (--shell), kicker/title (--accent/--text) use the NOLAN theme. Reveal is transform/opacity only (Python does the highlighting). Deferred modes: diff · scroll · char-level caret typing.

social_card.purpose: A social post / now-playing card overlay — X (Twitter) post, Reddit post, or Spotify now-playing. Collapses the registry's 7 social-overlay profiles toward ONE block + a `platform` param. The card slides + scales in; the Spotify progress bar fills.
social_card.when_to_use: Citing / quoting a real post in an explainer: a tweet, a Reddit thread, a track. The recognisable platform chrome IS the point — it reads as an authentic screenshot.
social_card.not_for: A generic quote (statement), a headline (newshead), a lower-third name tag (lower_third).

data_schema.platform: enum(x|reddit|spotify) — which platform's chrome + brand palette. Default x.
data_schema.name: string? — x: display name · reddit: (unused, uses subreddit) · spotify: (use track)
data_schema.handle: string? — x: @handle · spotify: falls back to artist
data_schema.text: string? — x: the tweet · reddit: the post title
data_schema.avatar: string? — x: avatar image path (else initials circle)
data_schema.likes: number? — x: likes · reddit: falls back for upvotes
data_schema.art: string? — spotify: album-art image path (else initials tile)
data_schema.progress: number? — spotify: play position 0..1 (bar fills to it). Default 0.4.
data_schema.position: enum(center|bl|br) — where the card sits. Default center.
data_schema.backdrop: string? — scene ground: a NOLAN token (default var(--shell)), a colour, or an image path.

social_card.note: BRAND-FIXED palette: each platform uses its OWN colours inline (Reddit orange #ff4500, X blue #1d9bf0, Spotify green #1db954) — NOT the NOLAN video theme (a Reddit card is always Reddit). Only the scene backdrop is NOLAN-themed. Reveal is transform/opacity only. Deferred platforms (same block): instagram/tiktok follow, youtube subscribe, macos notification.

spotlight.purpose: A background-removed SUBJECT (cutout image or video) placed center | left | right with a position-responsive LABEL: split to BOTH sides and sitting BEHIND a centered subject (the body overlaps the inner glyphs), or one block on the OPPOSITE side of a left/right subject. Deterministic layout — each label is region-bounded and auto-fits (never overflows its side), z-order is computed (label behind, subject in front), reveal is seek-safe (label spreads/slides in, subject fades + rises on top).
spotlight.when_to_use: A beat built around ONE person/object cutout as the hero with a short 1-2 part title framing it — the 'talking-head reel' look (a figure flanked by big kinetic words). The generalized form of the SpaceX/Falcon reel effect.
spotlight.not_for: A plain statement with no subject cutout (use statement), a news clipping (newshead), multiple images (collage/gallery), or a subject that must overlap arbitrary custom graphics (go raw).

data_schema.subject: string — asset path to a bg-removed cutout, image OR video (assets/x.png|.mp4). REQUIRED.
data_schema.src: string? — alias of `subject` (the bg-removed cutout asset path); `subject` takes precedence.
data_schema.position: enum(center|left|right) — where the subject sits; default center.
data_schema.words: array[str] (2-part label, one per side) OR a string (auto-split at its midpoint). The label.
data_schema.label: string? — alias of `words` (a 2-part label); `words` takes precedence if both set.
data_schema.kicker: string? — a small centered overline above the scene.
data_schema.behind: bool? — label behind the subject (default true; the center-subject overlap effect).
data_schema.motion: enum(rise|fade)? — subject entrance; default rise (fade+scale+move-up then slow float).
data_schema.label_layout: enum(overlap|clear)? — center only. default overlap (reel look: words centered, 47% each side, body overlaps inner glyphs). clear = auto-place words in the subject CLEAR zone (up + outside the silhouette), MEASURED from the cutout alpha so it adapts to any shape; deterministic + seek-safe; falls back loudly to overlap if not measurable (video/opaque/no clear band).
data_schema.label_valign: enum(auto|top|center)|number(0..1)? — clear mode only. vertical band for the words; auto=narrowest upper row (biased up); a number is a fraction of silhouette height from its top.
data_schema.clearance: number? — clear mode only. margin multiplier between word and silhouette (default 1.0).
data_schema.subject_scale: number(0..1)? — clear mode only. shrink the hero (default 1.0) to open a clear band.

constraints.notes: transforms/opacity only; label sizing is deterministic via data-fit.

hero.purpose: A cinematic ASYMMETRIC HERO (rule-of-thirds): a full-bleed image with the title anchored to ONE third and the subject breathing in the opposite two-thirds, over a directional scrim. The filmic opening / chapter card.
hero.when_to_use: A filmic OPENING or CHAPTER/section card: a portrait or establishing shot where one subject and a short title share the frame asymmetrically. Sets a scene or a mood.
hero.not_for: A symmetric centred statement (use `statement`/`stat`). A full-bleed image with no title (use `media_ground`). Labelling parts of the image (use `annotate`). A data device.

data_schema.side: enum(left|right)? — which third holds the text (default left); the subject breathes on the opposite side
data_schema.title: string | [lines] — the hero title (display type over the image)
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight with the accent
data_schema.kicker: string? — a small accent overline (a chapter no., a date/place)
data_schema.sub: string? — a one-line subtitle under the title
data_schema.kb: [from,to] | bool? — Ken-Burns scale on the image (default a gentle push)

hero.note: Title is white over a directional scrim (kicker + operative use --accent). Image Ken-Burns + text rise are seek-safe. src must be a real asset; keep the title short (a few words).

chat_thread.purpose: A message / DM EXCHANGE: chat bubbles alternating by sender, revealed in sequence (modern discourse, receipts, a conversation).
chat_thread.when_to_use: A beat that shows a CONVERSATION / exchange: a DM thread, a text argument, receipts of who-said-what, a back-and-forth.
chat_thread.not_for: A single quote (use pull_quote). A social post (use social_card). Narration on screen (the caption track carries speech).

data_schema.messages: array of {from?(them|me | a name), text, time?} — the bubbles, in order (me = accent right, them = surface left)
data_schema.subject: string? — a header name/title for the thread
data_schema.title: string? — a header title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to highlight

chat_thread.note: Bubbles: me = --accent (right), them = --surface (left). Keep 3-7 short messages; long threads overflow.

connection_board.purpose: A NETWORK / evidence board: nodes (people/events) joined by lines (the 'red-string' web — relationships, who-knew-whom, how things connect). Links draw, nodes pop.
connection_board.when_to_use: A beat about how things CONNECT / a web of relationships: follow-the-money, who-knew-whom, an investigation board, a system's parts and their links.
connection_board.not_for: A TREE / hierarchy (each node has ONE parent — org chart, ownership, taxonomy) → use `diagram` (tree). A one-way FLOW A→B→C → `diagram` (flow) or `timeline`. A CHAIN with no cross-links → `timeline`/`cycle`. connection_board earns its keep ONLY when links CROSS — a node connects to several and the web can't be drawn as a single parent-per-node tree. Ranked items → bullet_list/ledger.

data_schema.nodes: array of {id, label, sub?, x?, y? (0..1; else auto on a ring), hl?} — the entities
data_schema.links: array of {from, to (node ids), label?} — the connections (drawn lines, optional mid-label)
data_schema.kicker: string? — eyebrow over the top title
data_schema.title: string? — a top title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring to highlight

connection_board.note: Nodes are theme --surface cards (hl = accent border); links are accent lines (strokeDashoffset draw). Give x,y for a deliberate layout, else auto-ring. 3-7 nodes.

spans.purpose: A SPAN / ERA timeline: overlapping DURATION bars on a shared time axis (reigns, empires, projects, a Gantt), so 'these overlapped' reads at a glance. Bars grow from their start.
spans.when_to_use: A beat about DURATIONS that genuinely OVERLAP in time — empires/reigns/movements that coexisted, project phases that ran concurrently, lifespans, 'X was still going when Y began'. The point is the OVERLAP (who was contemporary with whom). If the bars run one-after-another with no overlap, it's a sequence, NOT spans.
spans.not_for: A sequence of point EVENTS or non-overlapping periods (use `timeline` — spans with no overlap is just a timeline drawn wrong). A single duration/countdown. Values to compare (chart). One-axis positioning (spectrum).

data_schema.spans: array of {label, start, end (numbers on the axis), hl?} — the duration bars, one row each
data_schema.range: [min, max]? — the axis extent (else min start → max end)
data_schema.ticks: array of numbers? — axis tick positions (else 5 even)
data_schema.unit: string? — appended to tick labels (e.g. ' AD')
data_schema.title: string? — a top-left title
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring to highlight
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills spans from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {label:'col', start:'col', end:'col'} — columns → each span's label + start/end

spans.note: Bars are theme --accent (0.5 opacity so overlaps read; hl = full); start/end are plain numbers (negative = BC). 2-6 rows read best.

data_table.purpose: A dataset as a full TABLE (columns × rows) with ONE cell SPOTLIGHTED — the number IN CONTEXT of its whole series. Rows reveal across the window; the highlighted row lifts and pulses at its cue.
data_table.when_to_use: A one-line beat that NAMES a single value ('4.4% in 2023') but you want to show it inside the full series/table so the viewer sees the trend and neighbours, not a bare stat. A small reference table (≤~10 rows) where one row is the point.
data_table.not_for: A single headline number with no context (use stat). A big comparison matrix of labels (use comparison_table). >~12 rows (too dense to read — filter the dataset first).

data_schema.columns: array of strings — column headers (auto-filled from a bound dataset's encode.columns)
data_schema.rows: array of rows, each an array of cell strings (auto-filled from a bound dataset's cells — provenance)
data_schema.highlight: object? — the spotlighted cell: {row, col} (0-based) OR {where:{col:value}} which the resolver maps to the matching row + the value column, so a one-line beat spotlights the exact number it names
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id; with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills columns/rows from real cells + stamps value_source (A-P1 gate passes)
data_schema.encode: object? — {columns:[...], value:'col'} — which columns to show + which is the value column (for suffix + highlight default)
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to accent

trajectory.purpose: A CONNECTED SCATTER — points in 2-D (x,y) joined by a path, revealed in order: a trajectory THROUGH a space over time.
trajectory.when_to_use: Two quantities moving together over time (inflation vs unemployment by year; a Phillips curve; loss vs step). A path that loops/reverses (a line chart can't show that).
trajectory.not_for: A single series vs time (use chart line + playhead). Categorical comparison (chart bar).

data_schema.points: array of {x, y, label?, cue?} — the ordered points (auto-filled from a dataset via encode x/y/label)
data_schema.title: string?

stream.purpose: A STACKED AREA / streamgraph — several series stacked into bands over an x axis, revealed by a left→right sweep: a COMPOSITION changing over time.
stream.when_to_use: How a whole splits into parts ACROSS time (energy mix by year, market share over time, budget composition).
stream.not_for: A single total over time (chart line). Parts of ONE moment (pie).

data_schema.series: array of {label, values:[numbers]} — the stacked bands (bottom→top)
data_schema.x: array of strings — the x-axis step labels
data_schema.title: string?

bar_race.purpose: A BAR RACE — horizontal bars for categories that GROW and REORDER across time steps, with a big period ticker: 'who's ahead over time'.
bar_race.when_to_use: A ranking that CHANGES over time (spend by company per year, cumulative counts, leaderboard evolution). The reordering IS the story.
bar_race.not_for: A static ranking at one moment (chart bar). A single series (chart line).

data_schema.series: array of {label, values:[v per step]} — each category's value at each step
data_schema.steps: array of strings — the time-step labels (drives the period ticker)
data_schema.title: string?

split_view.purpose: SPLIT SCREEN — one side a document page (cropped to a region so it tracks the narrative), the other any content (a video clip, a still, a text block, or a stat).
split_view.when_to_use: A beat that plays the PAPER against something else: the source region beside your explanation / a clip / a headline number. 'Here's what it says ∥ here's what it means.'
split_view.not_for: A full-frame page (use document). A visual A-vs-B of two images (use comparison).

data_schema.paper: object {document, page?, focus?} — the paper side; the resolver fills source/page_size/focus_rect from the ingested document (B-P1). focus = a region id to crop to.
data_schema.right: object {kind:image|video|text|stat, src?|text?|lines?|title?|value?|label?} — the OTHER side's content
data_schema.split: number? — the paper side's width fraction (0.25..0.75, default 0.5)
data_schema.paper_side: enum(left|right)? — which side the paper is on (default left)

slope.purpose: A SLOPE chart — each series is a line from its START value (left axis) to its END value (right axis); rank reversals/crossings are the reveal.
slope.when_to_use: Who overtook whom between TWO moments (2016 vs 2024; before vs after; forecast vs actual) — a ranking change across many series where the crossing IS the point.
slope.not_for: A single trend over many points (chart line + playhead). A magnitude gap at one moment (dumbbell). Time-series bands (stream).

data_schema.series: array of {label, start, end} — each series' value at the two moments (auto-filled from a dataset via encode {label,start,end})
data_schema.cols: array [leftLabel, rightLabel]? — the two moment headers over the axes
data_schema.highlight: int? — index of the series to ACCENT; the rest recede (the overtake you're narrating)
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string? — substring of title to accent
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id (project datasets/index.json); with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills series from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes). Prefer this over hand-typed numbers.
data_schema.query: object? — shape the table before encoding: filter/aggregate/sort/derive/top_n/select (see data verbs). Deterministic.
data_schema.encode: object? — {label:'col', start:'col', end:'col'} — column → each series' name + its start/end value

isotype.purpose: A PICTOGRAM / UNIT chart — a quantity as a grid of unit icons (each = N), so a big number becomes tangible and countable.
isotype.when_to_use: Making a large or abstract number FELT (each icon = 1M people; 8 GPUs; 73 of 100), or a countable comparison between a few categories. The Isotype/Neurath move.
isotype.not_for: A precise trend (chart). Many categories (bar chart). A part-of-whole percentage where one shape suffices (pie/stat).

data_schema.items: array of {label, value} — each category + its magnitude (auto-filled from a dataset via encode {value,label})
data_schema.per: number? — units ONE icon represents (auto-scaled to ~44/row if omitted)
data_schema.unit: string? — what one icon is (people, GPUs) — shown in the legend
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string?
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id; with `query`+`encode` the resolver fills items from real cells + stamps value_source (the A-P1 number gate passes).
data_schema.encode: object? — {value:'col', label:'col'} — column → each item's magnitude + label

dumbbell.purpose: A DUMBBELL / dot-plot — two values per category joined by a bar; the GAP between the dots is the point.
dumbbell.when_to_use: A gap between two conditions across categories (rich vs poor, promise vs reality, before vs after, men vs women) where the DISTANCE is the story.
dumbbell.not_for: A ranking reversal across two moments (slope). One value per category (chart bar). A magnitude as tangible units (isotype).

data_schema.items: array of {label, start, end} — each category + its two values (auto-filled from a dataset via encode {label,start,end})
data_schema.sort: bool? — order rows by the size of the gap (biggest first)
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string?
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id; the resolver fills items from real cells + stamps value_source (A-P1 gate).
data_schema.encode: object? — {label:'col', start:'col', end:'col'} — column → each row's label + its two values

small_multiples.purpose: SMALL MULTIPLES — a grid of tiny same-scale mini bar charts, one per panel, comparing the SHAPE across many categories at once.
small_multiples.when_to_use: 'The same pattern everywhere' (or 'everywhere but one'): a repeated small chart across countries/years/segments where the comparison of shapes IS the point. A shared y-scale.
small_multiples.not_for: A single series (chart). Two values per category (dumbbell). A ranking reversal (slope).

data_schema.panels: array of {label, series:[{label, value}]} — one mini chart per panel (auto-filled from a dataset via encode {panel,x,y}: rows GROUPED by the panel column)
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string?
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id; the resolver groups rows into panels + stamps value_source (A-P1 gate).
data_schema.encode: object? — {panel:'col', x:'col', y:'col'} — the column to SPLIT panels by + each mini bar's label/value

histogram.purpose: A HISTOGRAM / distribution — the SHAPE of a spread (bars touching), with an optional marker for 'where you fall'.
histogram.when_to_use: Showing a distribution — where the mass sits, how skewed/spread it is, and where one value falls on it ('most are here; you are there').
histogram.not_for: A few labelled categories (chart bar). A trend over time (chart line). A part-of-whole (pie).

data_schema.bins: array of {x0, x1, count} or {label, count} — the distribution (auto-filled from a dataset via encode {value}: the resolver BINS the raw column)
data_schema.marker: number? — a value on the axis to mark (where you fall / the mean)
data_schema.marker_label: string? — label for the marker (defaults to the value)
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string?
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id; the resolver bins the raw `value` column + stamps value_source (A-P1 gate).
data_schema.encode: object? — {value:'col', bins?:N} — the raw column to bin (+ optional bin count, default 12)

gauge.purpose: A GAUGE / progress ring — a value against a max as a sweeping radial arc with the number in the centre.
gauge.when_to_use: One (or a few) metric against a target/ceiling: progress to a goal, capacity used, a countdown, 'we're 73% there'. A punchy single-metric beat.
gauge.not_for: A precise trend (chart). Many categories (chart bar / isotype). A part-of-whole across categories (pie).

data_schema.items: array of {value, label} — one ring each (auto-filled from a dataset via encode {value,label}). Or give a single `value`+`label`.
data_schema.value: number? — shorthand for a single-ring gauge
data_schema.label: string? — single-ring label
data_schema.max: number? — the ceiling the arc fills toward (default 100)
data_schema.target: number? — a value to mark with a tick on the ring
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string?
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id; the resolver fills items + stamps value_source (A-P1 gate).
data_schema.encode: object? — {value:'col', label:'col'} — column → each ring's value + label

process.purpose: A PROCESS FLOW — linear steps connected by arrows, revealed in order (step 1 → 2 → 3): a pipeline / how-it-works.
process.when_to_use: An ordered PROCESS or how-it-works ('input → encode → attend → decode → output'; a 3-5 step method). An OPEN chain.
process.not_for: A repeating loop (cycle). A branching hierarchy (diagram). A flow whose WIDTH carries magnitude (sankey).

data_schema.steps: array of {label, sub?} — the ordered steps (auto-filled from a dataset via encode {label, sub?})
data_schema.title: string?
data_schema.titleHi: string?
data_schema.dataset: string? — bind a dataset id; the resolver fills steps + stamps value_source (A-P1 gate).
data_schema.encode: object? — {label:'col', sub?:'col'} — column → each step's label (+ optional sub-label)

media_ground.purpose: Full-bleed scene ground: image (dimmed + Ken-Burns + scrim) / paper (flat mist or parchment) / transparent (scrim only, reveals a root video behind).
media_ground.attached_via: scene data.ground = {kind: paper|image|transparent, parchment?, src?, kb?:[from,to], grade?, treatments?}
media_ground.treatments: optional [effect-id | {id, opacity?}] — stackable visual effects from the effects umbrella (colour grades, grain, film damage, fire/rain overlays). Validated against nolan.effects.registry; see `nolan capabilities -u effects` for the catalog.

prop_cutout.purpose: Object-as-evidence photo card (cash, GPU, bill, gavel, glass) stacked over a scene on its own track, small scale+settle reveal.
prop_cutout.attached_via: scene data.props = [{src, corner: br|bl|tr|tl, width, cue, tilt?, caption?}]

reveals._doc: Text-entrance vocabulary for the `statement` block (data.reveal). gsapify.com 'Text & Typography' effects rewritten SEEK-SAFE (pure fn of timeline progress — no Math.random/setTimeout), so every frame renders deterministically. Keys MUST equal compose.REVEALS (check_catalog.py enforces). Operative highlight-sweep is supported for all styles and fires after the text lands.
reveals.rise: the whole line rises + fades up (the Vox default; unchanged legacy path)
reveals.word: words slide up with a slight tilt, staggered (word-by-word-slide)
reveals.flip: letters flip in on the X axis with depth (letter-3d-flip)
reveals.typewriter: letters appear left->right behind a caret (typewriter)
reveals.scramble: letters cycle glyphs then resolve L->R, deterministic (text-scramble)
reveals.decode: katakana/hex glyphs decode into the text L->R (matrix-decode)
reveals.gradient: an accent gradient wipes the text in L->R (gradient-text-reveal)
reveals.glitch: RGB-split + jitter that settles to clean (glitch-text)

transitions._doc: Scene-to-scene transitions (the HF cover/push/scale/dissolve family, rewritten SEEK-SAFE). NOT a block: an optional `transition_out` on the DEPARTING scene ({kind, dur?, color?}) — an outgoing effect over an overlap window after the scene boundary, while the next scene does its own normal entrance underneath. Transforms/opacity only (literal CSS transition:/@keyframes are runtime-banned). Keys MUST equal compose.TRANSITIONS (check_catalog.py enforces).
transitions.crossfade: departing scene dissolves to reveal the next (HF dissolve)
transitions.slide_left: departing scene slides off to the left, uncovering the next (cover/push)
transitions.slide_right: departing scene slides off to the right, uncovering the next
transitions.slide_up: departing scene slides up out of frame, uncovering the next
transitions.scale_out: departing scene zooms up + fades (scale dissolve)
transitions.fade_through: dip through a colour (default black) between scenes; data.color overrides

clip_transitions._doc: FRAME-to-frame clip-driven transitions (matte/reveal WIPES between two rendered frame clips). A FRAME-level `transition_out` ({kind, dur?}) on a frame = the wipe INTO the next frame, spliced at the concat seam (nolan.hyperframes.transitions.transition_segment + incremental.splice_transitions). Distinct from a SCENE's `transition_out` above (within-frame GSAP, no pixel compositing). NET-ZERO: dur/2 is trimmed from each side + a dur segment (carrying that exact audio) inserted, so the global timeline / captions / BGM stay in sync (narration-owns-duration safe). Kinds are DATA, not code — they come from projects/_library/transitions/transitions.json (add via `nolan effects add-transition`), so this section is a doc, not a static key list; the live set is `nolan.hyperframes.transitions.transition_kinds()` (author.py gates the frame field against it). Two mechanisms: luma (ink/liquid matte -> maskedmerge) + chroma (green-screen reveal, e.g. fire, keyed foreground screened on top).

reveal_chars._doc: data.reveal_char — the ENTRANCE PERSONALITY of a data block's marks (the ease + how long the motion breathes). The scheduler owns WHEN a reveal fires; the character owns HOW. Meaning picks it: a shock number snaps, a climbing trend builds. Default 'settle'. Distinct from `reveal` (per-letter TEXT style).

settle.purpose: a confident, lightly-overshooting land — the default voice of a data reveal
settle.when_to_use: most reveals; a value arriving with quiet authority
settle.not_for: a hard shock (snap) or a slow trend (build)

snap.purpose: a hard, fast arrival with no overshoot — it just SLAMS into place
snap.when_to_use: a stark or shocking single number the narration hits hard
snap.not_for: many elements in sequence or a gentle trend (reads aggressive)

build.purpose: a slow, even accumulation that runs the length of its beat
build.when_to_use: a trend climbing, a cumulative total, a value that GROWS as the point develops

drift.purpose: a soft, atmospheric fade — barely-there motion
drift.when_to_use: ambient / contextual data sitting under narration; a calm mood
drift.not_for: the emphatic number a beat lands on (too weak)

stamp.purpose: an emphatic pop with strong overshoot — a visual exclamation mark
stamp.when_to_use: a punchline figure, a reveal the VO leans on
